items.title: Risk factors and inheritance
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors and inheritance

  • Age >50 years is the main sporadic factor; first-degree family history doubles the risk.
  • Lynch syndrome (HNPCC) due to mutation in repair genes MLH1/MSH2/MSH6/PMS2: cumulative risk 70-80% for CRC, with microsatellite instability (MSI-H).
  • Familial adenomatous polyposis (PAF) due to germline APC mutation: >100 adenomatous polyps, risk >90% at 40 years if colectomy is not performed.
  • Chronic inflammatory bowel disease (ulcerative colitis of long duration >8-10 years) increases risk due to dysplasia associated with colitis.
  • Diet rich in red and processed meats, obesity, smoking and type 2 diabetes are modifiable factors.

items.title: Clinical presentation symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Clinical presentation symptoms

  • Rectorhae and change in bowel habit (>3 weeks) are the most frequent; tenesmus and string stools suggest rectal tumor.
  • Unexplained iron deficiency anemia in men or postmenopausal women requires ruling out right CRC (more silent tumors).
  • Abdominal pain type of colic, feeling of incomplete evacuation and involuntary weight loss are warning signs.
  • Intestinal obstruction (vomiting, bloating, constipation) or perforation are urgent presentations with a poor prognosis.
  • left CRC usually gives early obstructive symptoms; the right presents with anemia and palpable mass.

items.title: Anatomical location
items.contentHtml:
Most common anatomical location

  • The sigmoid colon is the most common location (25-30% of all CRCs).
  • The rectum represents 30-35% overall if the rectosigmoid junction is included; in men it is more common.
  • The right colon (cecum and ascending) is more common in patients >70 years old and in Lynch syndrome.
  • The distribution has shifted towards the right colon in recent decades, possibly due to the use of aspirin and screening.
  • Serrated polyps Sessile are preferentially located in the right colon and have a carcinogenic pathway due to BRAF mutated.

items.title: Fecal occult blood screening
items.contentHtml:
Screening with fecal occult blood

  • SOHi (fecal occult blood immunochemistry) is the recommended population screening test; detects human hemoglobin with a threshold >10-20 µg/g of feces, without dietary restriction.
  • SOHg (guaiac) requires a diet without red meat or vitamin C; Its sensitivity is lower (50-60% vs. 80-85% for immunochemistry).
  • It is performed every 1-2 years in adults aged 50-75 years with average risk; if it is positive, colonoscopy is indicated.
  • The stool DNA test (Cologuard) combines detection of KRAS and BMP3/NDRG4 methylation with SOHi; Sensitivity 92% for CRC, but lower specificity (87%).
  • SOHi reduces mortality from CRC by 15-33% in randomized studies.

items.title: Gold standard colonoscopy
items.contentHtml:
Colonoscopy as the gold standard

  • Colonoscopy is the gold standard for diagnosis and treatment of premalignant lesions; sensitivity >95% for CRC.
  • Allows biopsy, polypectomy and Indian ink tattoo for surgical localization.
  • The adenoma detection rate (ADT) should be ≥25% in men and ≥15% in women as a quality indicator.
  • The interval screeningif normal colonoscopy is 10 years; if 1-2 low-grade tubular adenomas <10 mm are found, it is repeated after 5-10 years.
  • Adequate bowel preparation (Boston ≥6 or Ottawa <7) is essential; incomplete colonoscopy (due to poor preparation or stenosis) requires CT colonography or virtual colonoscopy.

items.title: TNM and Dukes staging
items.contentHtml:
TNM and Dukes staging

  • The TNM (AJCC 8th ed.) is the standard system: T1 invades submucosa, T2 muscularis propria, T3 subserosa, T4 perforates peritoneum/neighboring organs.
  • N1: 1-3 regional ganglia; N2: ≥4 ganglia. lymphovascular invasion worsens the prognosis.
  • Dukes A = T1-2N0M0 (limited to wall); Dukes B = T3-4N0M0; Dukes C = any T, N+; Dukes D = M1.
  • The Dukes is still used in some contexts but the TNM is mandatory for therapeutic decisions.
  • The pathological staging requires ≥12 lymph nodes examined to be adequate.

items.title: Tumor markers CEA and CA 19-9
items.contentHtml:
Tumor markers CEA and CA 19-9

  • The CEA (carcinoembryonic antigen) has 60-80% sensitivity in CRC, but low specificity; It is not used for screening.
  • It is used for post-treatment monitoring: if it rises after surgery, it suggests recurrence; normal value <5 ng/mL.
  • CA 19-9 is less sensitive (40-50%) and is elevated in left colon tumors and liver metastases; also in pancreatitis and cholestasis.
  • Preoperative elevation of CEA is associated with a worse prognosis; if >10 ng/mL, higher risk of relapse.
  • Routine CA 19-9 is not recommended; It is used in combination with CEA for monitoring metastatic disease.

items.title: Surgical treatment according to stage
items.contentHtml:
Surgical treatment according to stage

  • Stage 0 (Tis): complete endoscopic polypectomy; if positive margins, local resection.
  • Stage I (T1-2N0M0): segmental colectomy with regional lymphadenectomy (minimum 12 nodes).
  • Stage II (T3-4N0M0): curative surgery; adjuvant treatment with chemotherapy only if high risk (T4, obstruction, perforation, poor differentiation). resectable liver/pulmonary, en bloc resection or two-stage resection; if not, palliation with systemic chemotherapy.

items.title: Adjuvant chemotherapy in stage III
items.contentHtml:
Adjuvant chemotherapy in stage III

  • Indicated in all stage III (N+) after curative surgery; reduces relapse by 30-40%.
  • Standard regimen: FOLFOX (5-FU, leucovorin, oxaliplatin) or CAPOX (capecitabine + oxaliplatin) for 6 months.
  • In patients > 70 years or with comorbidities, 5-FU/leucovorin can be used. alone or capecitabine monotherapy. Stage II microsatellite instability (MSI-H) contraindicates adjuvant 5-FU (no benefit, possible harm); in stage III, it is used the same but with checkpoint inhibitors under investigation.
  • Oxaliplatin causes cumulative peripheral neuropathy; if ≥grade 2 appears, it is suspended.

items.title: Liver metastases and resection
items.contentHtml:
Liver metastases and resection

  • Liver metastases are the most common (40-50% of CRC); Surgical resection offers 5-year survival of 40-60%.
  • Resectability criteria: metastases limited to the liver, preserved liver function, R0 resection (free margins) preserving at least 30% of parenchyma.
  • If not initially resectable, conversion chemotherapy (FOLFOX/FOLFIRI + anti-EGFR if RAS wild-type) can reduce size and allow surgery.
  • radiofrequency or microwave ablation is an alternative for <3 cm unresectable lesions.
  • Preoperative CEA >200 ng/mL and extrahepatic disease are poor prognostic factors.

items.title: Lynch syndrome and polyposis
items.contentHtml:
Lynch syndrome and polyposis

  • Lynch syndrome: germline mutation in MLH1/MSH2/MSH6/PMS2 or deletion of EPCAM; tumors with MSI-H and loss of IHC expression.
  • Amsterdam II criteria: ≥3 relatives with CRC or associated cancer (endometrium, ovary, ureter), ≥2 generations, 1 diagnosed <50 years old.
  • Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP): >100 adenomatous polyps; APC mutation; risk of CRC >90% at age 40; requires prophylactic colectomy (proctocolectomy or total colectomy with ileal pouch).
  • Attenuated PAF: <100 polyps, late presentation; management with endoscopic surveillance every 1-2 years.
  • In Lynch, colonoscopy begins at 20-25 years every 1-2 years; in PAF, at 10-12 years old.

items.title: Post-treatment follow-up and recurrence
items.contentHtml:
Post-treatment follow-up and recurrence

  • After curative surgery, CEA every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months up to 5 years.
  • Colonoscopy one year after surgery; if normal, repeat after 3 years, then every 5 years.
  • CT of the chest-abdomen-pelvis annually for 3-5 years to detect liver/pulmonary metastases.
  • local recurrence occurs in 5-10% (more in the rectum); the distant (liver, lung) in 30-40%.
  • If CEA rises without visible lesion, perform PET-CT or colonoscopy; if resectable metastasis is confirmed, surgery with curative intent.
